sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a follow-up visit after an interior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) with the placement of another stent.
The patient reports similar symptoms to their previous heart attack, including chest pain and shortness of breath.
The patient has a history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, and diabetes.
The patient has been experiencing leg swelling since discharge from the hospital.
The patient has been taking their medications as prescribed and has good insurance coverage.
The patient has been less strict with their diet since the last heart attack, consuming high-sodium foods like pizza and pepperoni.
The patient has been managing their diabetes with metformin and walking their dog daily.
Review of systems: The patient denies any chest pain or shortness of breath.
Current medications:
Aspirin 81 milligrams daily
Brilinta 90 milligrams twice daily
Lipitor 80 milligrams daily
Toprol 50 milligrams daily
Metformin (for diabetes)
Lisinopril 20 milligrams daily
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are good, but the blood pressure is not specifically mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's neck exam is unremarkable with no jugular venous distention or carotid bruits.
The heart exam reveals a 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base.
The lungs are clear.
The patient has lower limb edema with pitting edema on the right radial artery, 1+ on the right radial artery.
The cath site is clean, dry, and intact without hematoma, and the right radial artery pulses are palpable.
Test Results: The patient's EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm, good R wave progression, and expected changes after a STEMI.
The echo reveals a reduced ejection fraction of 35%.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has coronary artery disease, newly reduced left ventricular dysfunction, and moderate mitral regurgitation, and acute heart failure.
Problem list: Coronary artery disease; reduced left ventricular dysfunction; moderate mitral regurgitation; acute heart failure.
Plan:
I prescribed Lasix 40 milligrams once daily for fluid retention.
I added Aldactone 12.5 milligrams daily for the same reason.
I referred the patient to cardiac rehab for education and exercise counseling.
I want the patient to continue their current medications, including aspirin, Brilinta, Lipitor, Toprol, and Metformin.
I will repeat an echo in about two months.
I also recommend nutrition counseling to discuss diet options, including cutting out preserved meat and finding alternatives.
Follow-up: The patient will be monitored over time, and another echo will be repeated in two months.
